name: Fašiangy (Slovak Shrovetide / Pre-Lenten Carnival)
city: Nationwide (major Slovak-American community events also in USA)
region: Slovakia (all regions); Slovak diaspora communities in USA via FCSU
dates: From Epiphany (January 6) through to Ash Wednesday; peak on the last three days (Sunday–Tuesday) before Ash Wednesday (Mardi Gras equivalent)
description: Fašiangy is the Slovak pre-Lenten carnival season, spanning from Epiphany (January 6) to Ash Wednesday — one of the longest Shrovetide seasons in European tradition. The word 'Fašiangy' derives from the German 'Fasching'. The season is associated with: masquerade processions through villages (costumed figures visit homes bringing luck and blessings); music, feasting, and dancing; traditional foods including pork, doughnuts (šišky), and sausages; and the symbolic 'burial of the bass' (pohreb basy) on the final Tuesday night, in which a bass instrument is ceremonially buried to mark the end of the party season and beginning of Lent. Rural Slovak communities traditionally held processions where men in straw or animal costumes, including bears (medveď), storks, and death figures, visited farmhouses. The First Catholic Slovak Union (FCSU) in the USA preserves and promotes Fašiangy traditions among Slovak-American communities.
